title: The Pillay Family Trail: Westcott Village Detectives
blurb: While Dad's at the Village Hall, become Westcott Detectives! Hunt down the secret weathervane, a hidden war memorial, an old church clock and the village's last pub. Isaac cracks the clues, Eli's got a spotting mission at each stop. It flexes: the village core is a comfy ~45 min with the buggy; add the woodland loop to stretch it toward 2 hours.

start.name: Westcott Village Hall (1 Institute Road, RH4 3NP)
start.directions: Park at the Village Hall (~22 spaces; overflow on Westcott Heath Road). ⚠️ SAFETY: the Green, dovecote, war-memorial shelter and the pub all sit right on the A25 main road — use the ZEBRA CROSSING by the Green and hold little hands. CORE (stops 1–5): village pavements, buggy-friendly, ~45 min. EXTENSION (stop 6): leaves the pavement onto a muddy bridleway/Greensand Way toward the Rookery — all-terrain buggy or a sling for Eli, and mind the stream. Turn back whenever little legs have had enough.

stops.name: The Secret Weathervane
stops.clue: On the Green stands a little thatched tower — a house for doves! Look UP at the weathervane spinning on top. Instead of N, E, S, W, a cheeky letter's been swapped so it spells a secret 4-letter word. What word does it spell?
stops.answer_hint: It's the start of the village's name… WEST-cott!
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find the little house for the birds, and flap your wings like a bird? 🕊️
stops.grownup: The dovecote's weathervane has its 'N' replaced with a 'T' so it reads WEST — for Westcott. Given to the village in 1922.
stops.story: This thatched dovecote (a home for doves) has sat on Westcott's Green for over 100 years. Its weathervane is a joke — the N is swapped for a T so it proudly spells WEST instead of pointing north!

stops.name: The Hidden War Memorial
stops.clue: Right next to the dovecote is a pretty thatched shelter — but it's secretly a war memorial. Find the plaque and read it carefully. What YEAR was the brave young soldier killed?
stops.answer_hint: It was at the Battle of the Somme — nineteen-sixteen.
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find the little house with the straw roof, and have a sit down? 🏠
stops.grownup: A thatched shelter given in 1922 by John Geake in memory of his son, Lt Boyd Burnet Geake, killed on 1 July 1916 at the Battle of the Somme.
stops.story: This sweet little shelter is really a war memorial. A dad gave it to the village to remember his son Boyd, who didn't come home from the First World War. We remember him every time we shelter here.

stops.name: The Queen's Clock
stops.clue: Walk through the little wooden-roofed gate (it's called a 'lych-gate') to the flint church. High up there's a clock, built for a Queen's special party — 50 years on the throne! Which Queen was it?
stops.answer_hint: There's a whole olden-days era named after her — Queen V…
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you walk through the special wooden gate, and point at the pointy church tower? ⛪
stops.grownup: Holy Trinity (1851) was designed by Sir George Gilbert Scott. The clock was added in 1887 for Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee; the lych-gate followed ~1890. All Grade II listed.
stops.story: This flint church is called Holy Trinity. Its clock was put up in 1887 to celebrate Queen Victoria being queen for 50 whole years — that's a Golden Jubilee!

stops.name: The Oldest Little Church
stops.clue: This little chapel (St John's) is the OLDEST building still used in the whole village. It opened in 1840. The big flint church you just saw opened in 1851. So is this little one OLDER or YOUNGER than the big church?
stops.answer_hint: 1840 came before 1851 — so the smaller number is…
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you find the little old church and give the door a gentle knock? 🚪
stops.grownup: St John's Free Church, founded 1840 — the oldest community building still standing in Westcott, predating Holy Trinity by over a decade.
stops.story: St John's has been here since 1840 — that makes it the oldest building in Westcott that people still use. Even older than the big church up the road!

stops.name: The Last Pub Standing
stops.clue: Long ago Westcott had LOTS of pubs — now only ONE is left! Find it (a good spot for a snack and a wee stop). It's named after a royal prince — what's it called?
stops.answer_hint: Look at the swinging sign — 'The Prince of …'
stops.little_ones: Eli — snack time! Can you spot the pub's sign swinging in the breeze? 🪧
stops.grownup: The Prince of Wales (Fuller's) is the village's only surviving pub — Westcott once had several. It reopened after a fire in 2023.
stops.story: This is the Prince of Wales — the very last pub left in Westcott. A good place to refuel before the woodland part of the adventure!

stops.name: The Stream of Six Mills (woodland extension)
stops.clue: Find the big stone gateposts that lead into the woods (The Rookery). A little stream here, Pipp Brook, was once so busy it turned the wheels of lots of watermills. How many mills did it once power — three, six, or ten?
stops.answer_hint: More than three but less than ten… count to 6!
stops.little_ones: Eli — find the big stone gateposts and count them: one… two! Then find the stream for a game of Pooh-sticks. 🌳
stops.grownup: Stone pillars mark Rookery Drive (a bridleway on the Greensand Way). Pipp Brook here once powered six watermills before joining the River Mole. From the pillars, follow the Greensand Way into the valley as far as you fancy — that's where the extra hour goes.
stops.story: These gateposts lead into The Rookery woods. The little stream, Pipp Brook, once powered six watermills — now it's perfect for Pooh-sticks. Wander the path as far as little legs allow, then head back.

finale.title: Case closed, Detectives Isaac & Eli! 🕵️🎉
finale.message: You cracked the secret weathervane, found the hidden war memorial, the Queen's clock, the oldest church and the last pub — proper Westcott Village Detectives. Show Mum and Dad your stickers!
finale.reward_idea: An ice cream, or a treat from the Prince of Wales — detectives' orders! 🍦

credits: Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors · NaPTAN · facts © Wikipedia (CC BY-SA) / Dorking Museum / Historic England
_recce_notes: FOOT-VERIFY before relying on geofences: Stop 6 Rookery pillars (~51.2232,-0.3768, med — bridleway centreline) and the Village Hall start door (~51.2248,-0.3674). HIGH-confidence (safe to trust): dovecote (1), war-memorial shelter (2), Holy Trinity (3), St John's (4), Prince of Wales (5). SAFETY: stops 1,2,5 are on the A25 — zebra crossing by the Green; controlled crossing near The Crown. The Crown & The Cricketers are CLOSED pubs — not used. Extension leaves pavement (mud/bridleway/stream).
